macro_segments.title: The Case of Issur Mitzvah vs. Issur Ervah in the Context of Achos Zekukaso (Sisters-in-Law Restrictions)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Case: Four Brothers, Two Sisters, and the Problem of Achos Zekukaso
The Distinction Between Issur Mitzvah Derabbanan and Real Issur Ervah
The Hava Amina: Can an Issur Derabbanan Solve a Derabbanan Problem?
The Gemara's Rejection: Mide'oraita Zika Remains Despite Derabbanan Status
The Principle of Mishum Mitzvah: Why Rabbanan Would Not Override the Zika Problem

macro_segments.title: Variations of the Basic Case: One Ervah vs. Two Ervos, and the Role of the Second Brother

micro_segments.title: Why Both Cases Are Needed: The Pedagogical Function of Showing Both One and Two Ervos
The Case of One Ervah: How It Establishes the Special Circumstance
The Case of Two Ervos: Mutual Proof and the Role of Sheni
The Distinction: When Sheni Proves vs. When It Does Not

macro_segments.title: Application to All Fifteen Arayos: The Debate Between Rav Yehuda and Abayei

micro_segments.title: The General Principle: All Fifteen Arayos Can Be Applied to the Sister-in-Law Case
Rav Yehuda's Restriction: The First Six Cases (Daughter and Variants) Are Excluded
The Problem with Daughter Cases: Requiring Out-of-Wedlock Birth
Abayei's Expansion: Even Daughter Cases Work, Whether Ba'onsin or Be'nisuin
Abayei's One Exception: Eishet Achiv She'Lo Hayah Be'Olamo (Brother's Wife When He Was Not Yet Born)

macro_segments.title: The Complex Scenario of Meis, Nolad, V'Yibem: Illustrating Eishet Achiv She'Lo Hayah Be'Olamo

micro_segments.title: Setup: Two Sets of Brothers and Two Unrelated Women
Stage One: Reuven Dies, Yissachar Is Born, Creating Eishet Achiv She'Lo Hayah Be'Olamo
Stage Two: Levi Does Yibum with Rachel
Stage Three: Shimon Dies, Stage Four: Zevulun Is Born, Creating the Opposite Eishet Achiv for Leah
Stage Five: Yehuda Does Yibum with Leah; The Complete Cross-Pattern
Explanation: Why This Only Works According to Rabbi Shimon

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Three Brothers, Two Sisters-in-Law, with One Single Brother (Panui)

micro_segments.title: Basic Case: Three Brothers, Two Married to Sisters, One Single
Extension: Application to All Types of Arayos Relationships
Consensus Halacha: When One Is Ervah, the Other Can Do Yibum or Chalitza
Rabbi Shimon's Radical Position: Complete Exemption from Both Chalitza and Yibum

macro_segments.title: The Scriptural Basis for Rabbi Shimon's View and the Policy of Mishum Tzarot

micro_segments.title: The Prooftext: 'Do Not Take a Woman with Her Sister to Create Friction'
Rabbi Shimon's Application: When Women Become Tzarot (Co-Sisters-in-Law), Neither Can Enter Yibum
The Theological Distinction: For Rabbi Shimon, This Is De'Oraita, Not Derabbanan
The Hava Amina for Rabbi Shimon: Fear of Gezeirah from the General Case
Rejection of the Hava Amina: Even Rabbi Shimon Allows Yibum When One Is Ervah

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Issur Mitzvah According to Rabbi Shimon: Gezeirah to Prevent Misunderstanding

micro_segments.title: Why Issur Mitzvah Requires Chalitza Even for Rabbi Shimon: The Concern About Public Perception
The Scenario: If One Woman Is a Grusha to a Kohen, People Might Misunderstand the Reason
The Gezeirah: Both Women Must Get Chalitza to Avoid Confusion About Issur Mitzvah Cases Generally
Distinction from Real Ervah: Public Understanding Is Clearer When Ervah Status Is Obvious

macro_segments.title: New Case: Three Brothers, Two Sisters-in-Law, One Single Brother Who Does Ma'amar Before His Brother Dies

micro_segments.title: The Setup: Two Married, One Single; One Married Brother Dies and the Single Does Ma'amar with His Widow
The Question: Does Ma'amar Create a Strong Enough Marital Bond to Overcome Achos Zekukaso?
Beit Shammai's Position: Ma'amar Suffices; Rachel Becomes His Wife, Leah Walks Off
Beit Hillel's Position: Ma'amar Is Insufficient; Requires Both Get and Chalitza
The Consequences: According to Beit Hillel, Both Wives Are Harmed (Woe to Wife and Co-Wife)

macro_segments.title: Analysis of Beit Shammai: The Degree to Which Ma'amar Creates a Complete Kinyan

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar's Clarification: Ma'amar Is Not a Complete Kinyan (Koneh Kinyan Gamur) for All Purposes
The Test Case: If Divorce Were Sought, Would Only Get Suffice or Is Chalitza Needed?
The Distinction: Ma'amar Is Enough to Push Off Leah and Allow Consummation, But Not a Full Kinyan
Ma'amar Dachaya Derabbanan: Ma'amar Only Pushes Off the Co-Sister via Derabbanan Mechanism

macro_segments.title: The Logical Problem: Why Not Use Ma'amar to Solve the Achos Zekukaso Problem at the Outset?

micro_segments.title: The Question: In a Case of Achos Zekukaso, Why Not Do Ma'amar to Rachel First, Then Yibum?
The Answer: Ma'amar Deheitera (Permissible Ma'amar) vs. Ma'amar De'Issura (Prohibited Ma'amar)
The Principle: Ma'amar Only Works Meaningfully When Done Without a Competing Zika
Application: Once Leah's Zika Is on the Scene and Yibum with Rachel Is Prohibited, Ma'amar Cannot Create Full Marital Status

macro_segments.title: Remaining Question: The Precise Status of Ma'amar Deheitera—Is It 100% Kinyan or Less?

micro_segments.title: The Unresolved Issue: Does Ma'amar Create Full Marital Status Requiring Only Get, or Partial Status Requiring Chalitza?
Why It Matters: Whether Chalitza Is Needed If the Couple Later Seeks to Divorce
Acknowledgment: Debate Will Continue About the Full Weight of Ma'amar According to Beit Shammai
